-JPZ21IAAAAJ.keywords: robotics, visual servoing, image-based visual servoing, robot modeling and control, motion planning, sampling-based motion planning, multirobot systems, simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM), visual tracking, bioinspired aerial robotics
-JPZ21IAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Seth Hutchinson is a senior researcher and professor in **robotics** currently affiliated with *Northeastern University*, where he leads an active research group focused on integrating perception, planning, and control for robotic systems. His work centers on <u>visual servoing</u>, <u>robot modeling and control</u>, and <u>motion planning</u>, with a long-standing emphasis on connecting rigorous mathematical foundations to practical implementations. Hutchinson is best known for authoritative textbooks and tutorials that have shaped how researchers and practitioners approach **robot motion**, **visual feedback control**, and system-level design for multirobot and long-duration autonomy applications.

---

## Research Areas
Hutchinson’s research spans several tightly connected areas of robotics: foundational modeling and control of manipulators (exemplified by the textbook "Robot Modeling and Control"), vision-based feedback and *visual servo control* (notably the tutorial "A tutorial on visual servo control" and the two-part series "Visual servo control. I. Basic approaches" and "Visual servo control. II. Advanced approaches"), and algorithmic motion planning for single and multiple robots ("Principles of robot motion: theory, algorithms, and implementations." and work on optimal planning for multiple robots). He has contributed techniques for image-based and partitioned visual servoing ("A new partitioned approach to image-based visual servo control"; "Performance tests for visual servo control systems"), methods for planning under sensing and field-of-view constraints (e.g., homography-based controllers and FOV-constrained path planning), and approaches for real-time planning in changing environments (probabilistic roadmap–related planners and dynamic replanning). In recent years his interests have broadened to include long-duration autonomy and resilience in multi-robot teams ("Robot ecology: Constraint-based control design for long duration autonomy"; resilient and energy-aware task allocation), biologically inspired aerial robotics (bat-inspired platforms and Bat Bot), and modern data-driven/learning approaches to manipulation (e.g., vision–tactile grasping with transformers). Across these topics he repeatedly bridges theory (stability, optimality, planning complexity) with systems-level validation (real-time control, tracking, and experimental platforms).

---

## Notable Works
- "Robot Modeling and Control" (2006) — A widely used textbook that formalizes kinematics, dynamics, and control for robotic manipulators and provides implementation-oriented insights that have educated generations of robotics students and practitioners.
- "A tutorial on visual servo control" (2002) and the two-part series "Visual servo control. I. Basic approaches" (2006) / "Visual servo control. II. Advanced approaches" (2007) — Definitive tutorial and survey contributions that synthesized computer vision and control perspectives, established standard formulations for IBVS/PBVS, and influenced subsequent algorithmic and experimental work in vision-based robot control.
- "Principles of robot motion: theory, algorithms, and implementations." (2005) — A comprehensive treatment of motion planning theory and practical algorithms (including probabilistic roadmaps and sampling-based methods) that connects low-level implementation details to high-level planning concepts and has served as a reference for planners for both single and multi-robot systems.

---

## Academic Background
Seth Hutchinson is a highly cited and influential figure in robotics (total citations ~33,236), with a career spanning foundational textbook authorship, broad tutorial contributions, and experimental systems research dating to the late 1980s and 1990s. His publication record documents sustained contributions to vision-based control, motion planning, multirobot coordination, and bio-inspired aerial robotics. Hutchinson has held faculty and research appointments at major robotics centers (historical web presence associated with the Georgia Tech robotics community and extensive publication ties to University of Illinois researchers), and is currently affiliated with *Northeastern University*. He has supervised numerous students and collaborators, contributed to community education through textbooks and tutorials, and played influential roles in shaping both theoretical and applied directions in robotics research. For more about his publications and group activities, see his professional homepage.

1GsJvtwAAAAJ.keywords: Cyber Forensics, Systems Security, Malware Analysis, Memory Forensics, Android Forensics, Software Supply Chain Security, Package Manager Attacks, Symbolic Root Cause Analysis, ARM Binary Rewriting, Web Application Abuse (CMS-targeting)

1kW15d8AAAAJ.keywords: software engineering, science policy, articificial intelligence, software reuse, requirements engineering, software design, software maintenance, software engineering education, HGVS nomenclature, sequence variant validation

2--dZP0AAAAJ.keywords: Equity in CS Education, Coaching inclusive pedagogy, Broadening Participation in Computing, AP CS, AP CS Principles, K-12 Computer Science Education, Instructional Coaching for CS Teachers, Equity-Focused Research Methods, CS Curriculum Development, Social Justice in CS Education
2--dZP0AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Lien Diaz is the Director of the **Constellations Center for Equity in Computing** at **Georgia Tech**, where she leads efforts that center *equity-driven* change in computing education across K–12 and higher education contexts. Her scholarship and leadership focus on advancing <u>equity</u> and <u>broadening participation</u> in computing through research-practice partnerships, curriculum development, and educator support systems. Diaz’s work is particularly tied to the development and implementation of the **AP Computer Science Principles** initiative and to coaching models for *inclusive pedagogy* that aim to transform classroom practice and institutional policy.

---

## Research Areas
Diaz’s research spans the intersection of computer science education, policy, and equity. She examines how curriculum initiatives such as **AP Computer Science Principles** shape classroom practices, teacher preparation, and access for historically underrepresented students (see “CS principles: development and evolution of a course and a community” (2013) and the 2024 study on AP CSP’s role in driving equitable pedagogy). Her work develops and evaluates models for *coaching and instructional support* to improve CS teaching (e.g., “Coaching to Improve CS Teaching and Learning” (2022); descriptions of instructional coaches for elementary coding in 2025). Diaz also contributes to methodological and leadership frameworks for equity-focused K–12 CS research and practice — proposing shared visions for equity (“Going beyond the platitudes of equity” (2019)), leadership models for broadening participation (“Setting the table for equity” (2022)), and guiding recommendations for high-quality, equity-centered research in the field (2023). Across these topics she consistently centers systemic barriers, teacher capacity, and the design of curriculum and assessment to promote inclusive learning environments.

## Notable Works
- “Does the Advanced Placement Computer Science (CS) Principles course drive equitable and inclusive CS pedagogy, curriculum, and policy as a means to broaden participation in …” (2024) — an empirical and policy-oriented investigation of the extent to which AP CSP fulfills its aim to broaden participation and influence classroom and institutional practice.
- “Going beyond the platitudes of equity: Developing a shared vision for equity in computer science education” (2019) — a foundational piece articulating a concrete vision and actionable principles for making equity an operational priority in CS education research and implementation.
- “CS principles: development and evolution of a course and a community” (2013) — a descriptive and historical account of the AP CSP project that documents curriculum design choices, community-building efforts, and the intended role of the course in expanding access to computing.

## Academic Background
Lien Diaz’s scholarship is embedded in long-term collaborations with national curriculum and professional organizations (notably the **College Board** for AP initiatives) and in the SIGCSE/CS education research community, where many of her contributions have been presented. As Director of the Constellations Center for Equity in Computing at Georgia Tech, she leads research-practice partnerships and advises on large-scale efforts to transform CS instruction. Her publication record (total citations: **62**) shows sustained impact on policy-relevant curriculum development (AP CSP), equity frameworks, and educator coaching models. Diaz is a frequent author of conference workshops and working papers that bridge research, teacher professional learning, and K–12/university collaboration; she has partnered with organizations such as BootUp and other practitioner networks to translate equity-focused research into scalable professional learning and curricular resources. Her work has been recognized within the CS education community for shaping conversations about how to make computing education more inclusive, rigorous, and accessible to diverse learners.

2HLpBtQAAAAJ.keywords: Pedagogical democratization, algorithmic transparency, online graduate education, broadening CS research opportunities, student research motivations, inclusive CS education, computer programming training, learning management systems (LMS), educational equity, underserved communities
2HLpBtQAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Dante Ciolfi, MS, MEd, is a researcher affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology** whose work sits at the intersection of **computing education**, *online learning*, and community-focused instructional design. His scholarship and practice focus on <u>pedagogical democratization</u> and the role of transparent systems in enabling equitable access to research and training opportunities — synthesizing technical, pedagogical, and organizational perspectives to make CS research and programming education more accessible to non-traditional and distributed learners.

---

## Research Areas
Ciolfi’s research centers on expanding who participates in computing research and how those opportunities are structured. He investigates student motivations and interest formation in *graduate computer science research* (see "Examining Student Interest and Motivations in Graduate Computer Science Research", 2025) and designs programmatic models to broaden research access for *online graduate students* ("Broadening CS Research Opportunities for Online Graduate Students", 2025). Earlier work developed practical delivery and support approaches for free programming instruction aimed at <u>under-served groups</u>, such as the LMS/Support Model for distributed learners (2017–2018). Across these threads he emphasizes <u>pedagogical democratization</u> and the need for algorithmic and process-level transparency — studying how transparent policies, platform affordances, and support structures influence participation, agency, and equitable outcomes.

---

## Notable Works
- "Examining Student Interest and Motivations in Graduate Computer Science Research" (2025) — A study highlighting the mismatch between top-down research agendas and student-driven interests, proposing mechanisms to surface and integrate student motivations into lab and project selection.
- "Broadening CS Research Opportunities for Online Graduate Students" (2025) — A design- and evidence-focused paper that articulates scalable practices and program structures to connect *remote master’s students* with meaningful research experiences and mentoring.
- "Computer Programming Training for Under-Served Groups: the LMS/Support Model" (2017) — An early, practice-oriented contribution introducing a support-centered LMS approach to deliver free programming training to marginalized communities, laying groundwork for subsequent outreach and equity-focused interventions.

---

## Academic Background
Ciolfi holds an MS and an MEd from the **Georgia Institute of Technology** and remains affiliated with the institute in roles that bridge teaching, research, and outreach. His publication record (four papers, total citations: 4) traces a progression from community-centered training interventions to empirical and design work on graduate research access for online students. He has presented lightning talks and conference contributions on instructional models and has collaborated on projects that address equity, access, and transparency in computing education. A public homepage was not specified; his work is primarily visible through the listed publications and presentations.

2PxFftgAAAAJ.keywords: Information Visualization, Visual Analytics, Human-Computer Interaction, Algorithm Animation, Software Visualization, Interaction Design, Visualization Evaluation, Sensemaking, Natural Language Interfaces for Visualization, Fault Localization

2QsddMIAAAAJ.keywords: Materials discovery, Materials informatics, Computational Materials Science, AI for Science, Single-atom catalysts, Atomically precise metal nanoclusters, Density functional theory, Graph neural networks, Methane activation, Heterogeneous catalysis
2QsddMIAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Victor Fung is an **Assistant Professor** in **Computational Science and Engineering** at the Georgia Institute of Technology and leads the Fung Group (https://www.fung-group.org/). His research sits at the intersection of *computational materials science*, *catalysis*, and *materials informatics*, with a primary emphasis on accelerating **materials discovery** using data‑driven and first‑principles methods. The Fung Group combines high‑throughput density functional theory (DFT), *graph neural networks* and other machine learning approaches to tackle problems such as <u>single‑atom and atomically precise metal catalysts</u>, <u>electronic structure descriptors (density of states)</u>, and <u>inverse design of functional materials</u> for energy and chemical conversion.

---

## Research Areas
Fung’s work spans computational catalysis, nanochemistry, and AI‑enabled materials design. He has developed and applied DFT‑level investigations of metal nanoclusters and single‑atom catalysts — highlighting stability, electronic structure, and catalytic activity (e.g., atomically precise metal nanoclusters and single‑atom Pt/Rh on supports). He has advanced mechanistic understanding and design strategies for methane activation and oxidative dehydrogenation reactions on perovskites, oxides, and supported metals. On the materials‑informatics side, he has led benchmarking and method development for machine learning in materials chemistry (notably graph neural networks), produced physics‑informed ML models for electronic density of states and adsorption energies, and explored inverse‑design workflows (including invertible neural networks and LLM‑assisted discovery) for MOFs, 2D materials, polymers, and high‑entropy alloy nanoparticles. Across these areas his publications combine theory, data curation, and ML model evaluation to generate predictive descriptors and actionable design rules for catalysis and functional materials.

---

## Notable Works
- "Golden single-atomic-site platinum electrocatalysts" (2018) — Demonstrated synthesis/characterization principles and catalytic performance for single‑atomic‑site Pt electrocatalysts, advancing the field of **single‑atom catalysis** and providing a model system for tuning geometric and electronic effects in electrocatalysis.  
- "Insights into interfaces, stability, electronic properties, and catalytic activities of atomically precise metal nanoclusters from first principles" (2018) — A comprehensive first‑principles study linking atomic‑scale structure, ligand effects, and electronic properties to catalytic behavior in ligand‑protected metal nanoclusters, framing design rules for atom‑precise nanocatalysts.  
- "Benchmarking graph neural networks for materials chemistry" (2021) — A widely cited methodological contribution that systematically evaluates GNN architectures and datasets for materials problems, establishing best practices and highlighting capabilities/limitations of GNNs for property prediction and materials screening.

---

## Academic Background
Victor Fung leads an interdisciplinary research program embedded in Georgia Tech’s Computational Science and Engineering environment, where his group integrates computational chemistry, catalysis, and machine learning. His publication record — spanning high‑impact experimental‑theory collaborations on nanoclusters and single‑atom catalysts to methodological advances in materials informatics and GNN benchmarking — indicates extensive training at the interface of theory and experiment. Fung’s work has attracted broad attention (total citations > 6,000), reflecting influence across heterogeneous catalysis, nanochemistry, and AI for science. He maintains active collaborations with experimentalists and computational scientists, mentors graduate and postdoctoral researchers in multidisciplinary projects, and is associated with national and international materials chemistry and data‑science communities through coauthorships and dataset releases.

2a5XgNAAAAAJ.keywords: Machine Learning, Perception, Robotics, Artificial Intelligence, Few-Shot Learning, Continual Learning, Embodied AI, Semi-Supervised Object Detection, Out-of-Distribution Detection, 3D Perception and 6D Pose Estimation
2a5XgNAAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Zsolt Kira is an **Associate Professor** in the School of Interactive Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group that sits at the intersection of **machine learning**, **computer vision**, and **robotics**. His lab focuses on building scalable perception and learning systems for embodied agents and autonomous systems. His work emphasizes robust, generalizable methods for perception and continual adaptation in real-world settings — particularly in areas such as <u>few-shot and transfer learning</u>, <u>continual/lifelong learning</u>, <u>semi-supervised and out-of-distribution detection</u>, and <u>embodied AI / simulation for robot learning</u>. Across these themes he pursues both foundational theory (e.g., training dynamics and stability) and practical systems (e.g., simulation platforms and mobile manipulation), producing widely used benchmarks and algorithms. His scholarship has been highly cited (total citations ~15,901) and spans contributions to both algorithmic understanding and applied robotics systems.

---

## Research Areas
Kira’s research spans several tightly connected areas in modern AI and robotics. He has made influential contributions to *few-shot learning and meta-learning* (e.g., the empirically-focused critique and standardization in "A Closer Look at Few-shot Classification" that clarified evaluation practices), to the *optimization and stability of generative models* (e.g., reframing GAN training as regret-minimization in "On convergence and stability of gans"), and to *out-of-distribution detection* for deep vision systems (e.g., "Generalized ODIN"). In embodied and robotic perception he has advanced large-scale simulated training environments and benchmarks with works such as "Habitat 2.0" and "Habitat 3.0", enabling research on interactive rearrangement and human-robot cohabitation. He also addresses practical learning settings: *semi-supervised object detection* ("Unbiased Teacher"), *continual learning* (e.g., "Coda-prompt" and re-evaluations of continual scenarios), and *collaborative multi-agent perception* ("When2com", "Who2com"). Across these topics he often couples methodological innovation (architectures, training procedures, calibration) with strong empirical evaluation and publicly available platforms or benchmarks.

---

## Notable Works
- "A Closer Look at Few-shot Classification" (2019) — a highly-cited empirical study (≈2,834 cites) that standardized evaluation protocols and dissected sources of performance in few-shot benchmarks, substantially shaping subsequent meta-learning and few-shot research.
- "On convergence and stability of gans" (2017) — a theoretical and empirical reframing of GAN training dynamics as regret-minimization (≈963 cites) that advanced understanding of mode collapse and stability in generative models.
- "Habitat 2.0: Training Home Assistants to Rearrange their Habitat" (2021) — a systems and benchmark contribution (≈800 cites) that delivered a simulation platform and tasks for interactive embodied AI, enabling scalable training and evaluation of mobile manipulation and rearrangement agents.

---

## Academic Background
Zsolt Kira is an established researcher whose publication record stretches back over a decade, with influential papers across machine learning, perception, and robotics. As an Associate Professor at Georgia Tech he mentors students and collaborators on both theoretical and systems-level problems and contributes community resources (e.g., simulation platforms and benchmarks) that have accelerated embodied AI research. His body of work — reflected in highly-cited contributions to few-shot learning, GAN theory, semi-supervised detection, continual learning, and embodied simulation — has led to broad recognition in the fields of **machine learning**, **perception**, and **robotics**. He maintains an active lab and project page (https://faculty.cc.gatech.edu/~zk15) and is regularly involved in cross-disciplinary collaborations that translate algorithmic advances into practical robotic systems and community datasets.

4UZP8bMAAAAJ.keywords: Cryptography, Hardware Security, Cyber Physical System Security, Hardware IP Protection, Reverse Engineering, Design-Hiding, Optical Side-Channel Attacks, Industrial Control Systems Security, Maritime Cybersecurity, Device Authentication
4UZP8bMAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Animesh Chhotaray is a **Research Scientist** at **Georgia Tech**, working at the intersection of *cryptography*, *hardware security*, and *cyber-physical system security*. His work is situated in labs and collaborations that focus on practical, empirical security for electronics, industrial control, and cyber-physical infrastructure. Across his projects he emphasizes <u>practical attack discovery</u>, <u>design-hardening techniques</u>, and the systematic evaluation of cryptographic and hardware defenses, combining cryptanalysis, side-channel analysis, and large-scale empirical measurement to improve system resilience.

---

## Research Areas
Chhotaray’s research spans applied topics in **cryptography**, **hardware IP protection**, and **cyber-physical systems (CPS) security**. He has critically examined cryptographic practice in standards (notably the analysis of IEEE P1735), demonstrating how misplaced cryptographic choices and management policies can undermine the security of electronic-design intellectual property. In hardware security he studies <u>design-hiding</u> and protection against reverse-engineering, proposing principled approaches to evaluate and harden circuit-design IP. His CPS work covers both operational environments — from *field-deployed PLCs* and large-scale Internet scan analyses to the maritime domain — where he combines active measurement and human-centered studies (e.g., mariners’ perspectives) to surface attack vectors and defensive needs. Other threads include optical and sensor side-channels for intellectual-property theft (e.g., 3D-printing setups), device fingerprinting and authentication challenges, and educational efforts to integrate *cyber-informed engineering* into curricula.

---

## Notable Works
- "Standardizing Bad Cryptographic Practice: A Teardown of the IEEE Standard for Protecting Electronic-design Intellectual Property" (2017) — a detailed critique of **IEEE P1735** that uncovers systemic cryptographic errors and their practical implications for protecting electronic IP (29 citations).  
- "Hardening Circuit-Design IP Against Reverse-Engineering Attacks" (2022) — work that proposes a principled framework for evaluating and improving *design-hiding* techniques in electronic circuits, moving beyond ad hoc schemes toward measurable security guarantees (13 citations).  
- "A Sea of Cyber Threats: Maritime Cybersecurity from the Perspective of Mariners" (2025) — a cross-disciplinary study documenting operational cybersecurity challenges in maritime systems and highlighting human-technical gaps that must be addressed to secure shipping and port infrastructure (11 citations).

---

## Academic Background
Chhotaray has produced a body of work since at least 2017 that bridges applied cryptanalysis, hardware security, and cyber-physical systems research. Based at **Georgia Tech** as a Research Scientist, he collaborates across academia, industry, and operational communities to evaluate deployed systems and to translate findings into practical defenses and educational resources. His publications combine empirical measurement, red-team style evaluation, and standards analysis; he is also active in curriculum work, contributing to the *Cyber-Informed Engineering (CIE)* curriculum guide and adoption studies. With a cumulative citation record (61 citations) and a record of multi-domain contributions, his profile reflects a commitment to improving the security posture of hardware and CPS through rigorous analysis, tool development, and community-facing outputs. For more information, see his homepage: https://animeshchhotaray.github.io/.

5BWj5TgAAAAJ.keywords: AI Powered Computing Education, Scientific Computing, Porous Media, Intelligent Geotechnics, Image-based particle characterization, X-ray micro-computed tomography, Pore network analysis, Microbial-induced calcite precipitation (MICP), Landslide susceptibility mapping, Generative AI in software engineering education
5BWj5TgAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Dr. Nimisha Roy is a faculty member in the **College of Computing** at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, where she leads an interdisciplinary research program at the intersection of **computational methods** and *domain-driven engineering problems*. Her work combines high-resolution imaging, numerical analysis, and machine learning to study <u>particle morphology</u>, <u>pore space architecture</u>, and <u>AI-augmented computing education</u>. Roy’s portfolio spans experimental and computational *scientific computing* applied to granular and porous media as well as research on integrating **generative and applied AI tools** into computing curricula and assessment workflows. Her scholarly output (total citations: **288**) is documented on her professional homepage: https://nimisharoy9.wixsite.com/myportfolio.

---

## Research Areas
Roy’s research can be grouped into two tightly coupled thrusts. The first is quantitative characterization of granular and porous materials using digital imaging and tomographic data: she develops image-based techniques to extract particle shape metrics (sphericity, roundness, angularity and roughness) and to quantify pore bodies and throats from X‑ray micro-CT scans, then links these microstructural descriptors to macroscale mechanical and flow responses (see e.g., "Image based shape characterization of granular materials and its effect on kinematics of particle motion" (2018), "Quantifying three-dimensional bodies and throats of particulate system pore space" (2023), and "3-D contact and pore network analysis of MICP cemented sands" (2023)). The second thrust is *AI‑powered computing education and data-driven decision support*: she investigates practical integration of AI tools into advanced CS coursework and assessment (e.g., "Empowering Future Software Engineers: Integrating AI Tools into Advanced CS Curriculum" (2025), "VISGRADER: Automatic Grading of D3 Visualizations" (2023)), and explores machine learning for geohazard and disaster applications (positive‑unlabeled approaches for landslide susceptibility (2024), nighttime-light and social-media image analysis for disaster damage assessment (2023, 2025)). Methodologically, her work combines image processing, pore-network modeling, experimental tomography, supervised and weakly supervised ML, and curriculum redesign/education research.

---

## Notable Works
- "Image based shape characterization of granular materials and its effect on kinematics of particle motion" (2018, 114 citations) — Introduced a computational pipeline for robust quantification of particle shape features (sphericity, roundness, roughness) from images and demonstrated how those metrics affect particle kinematics and packing behavior, providing widely used tools for granular-material characterization.  
- "3-D contact and pore network analysis of MICP cemented sands" (2023, 14 citations) — Developed a 3D image-analysis framework for evaluating microstructural changes due to microbial‑induced calcite precipitation (MICP), linking contact geometry and pore-network evolution to observed mechanical strengthening in cemented sands.  
- "Empowering Future Software Engineers: Integrating AI Tools into Advanced CS Curriculum" (2025, 14 citations) — Presented an evidence-based curriculum redesign and pedagogical approach for incorporating AI tools into capstone and advanced software engineering courses, addressing assessment, tool literacy, and the shifting skillset required for modern software development.

---

## Academic Background
Roy’s publication record reflects an academic trajectory rooted in *geotechnical and computational characterization* of particulate materials with a subsequent and deliberate pivot into **computing education research and AI applications** in engineering and disaster science. As a faculty member at the Georgia Institute of Technology’s College of Computing, she operates at the boundary of engineering and computer science, advising students on projects that span experimental tomography, pore-network modeling, machine learning for hazard assessment, and AI‑augmented pedagogy. Her interdisciplinary contributions—evidenced by collaborative publications across geotechnics, remote sensing, and computing education and a cumulative citation count of **288**—have positioned her as a scholar who bridges domain expertise in porous media and granular mechanics with methodological advances in scientific computing and educational technology. Further information, publications, and student projects are available via her homepage: https://nimisharoy9.wixsite.com/myportfolio.

5HJzWMUAAAAJ.keywords: Mathematics, Computer Science, Random graphs, Probabilistic combinatorics, Random constraint satisfaction problems, Approximate counting and sampling, Spin systems and statistical physics, Phase transitions and information-theoretic thresholds, Belief propagation and cavity method, Expander graph algorithms

6WlU86IAAAAJ.keywords: OHDSI, OMOP Common Data Model, Electronic Health Records, Synthetic EHR generation, Generative Adversarial Networks, Variational Autoencoders, Clinical Decision Support, Drug–Drug Interactions, Comparative Effectiveness Research, Health Data Quality and Interoperability
6WlU86IAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Jon Duke is a senior researcher based at **Georgia Tech**, working at the intersection of **clinical informatics**, **health data science**, and *computational medicine*. He is an active contributor to the global Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics ecosystem and routinely collaborates with multi‑institutional networks and clinical partners. His primary research focus centers on the development of scalable methods and systems for extracting reliable evidence from real‑world health data — with particular emphasis on <u>observational health data networks</u>, <u>electronic health record (EHR) data standardization and quality</u>, and <u>privacy‑preserving synthetic data generation</u> to enable reproducible and shareable analyses.

---

## Research Areas
Duke’s work spans a blend of methodological, systems, and applied topics in biomedical informatics. He has helped shape the practice of large‑scale observational research through contributions to the OHDSI community (see "Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (OHDSI): opportunities for observational researchers"), including work on the transformation and quality assessment of heterogeneous EHRs into common data models ("Incrementally transforming electronic medical records into the Observational Medical Outcomes Partnership common data model", "Transforming French electronic health records into the Observational Medical Outcome Partnership's common data model"). He is a leader in privacy‑aware data sharing and synthetic data methods, developing generative approaches for EHR synthesis ("Generating multi‑label discrete patient records using generative adversarial networks", "EVA: Generating longitudinal electronic health records using conditional variational autoencoders"). His papers also address *explainable* clinical NLP and predictive modeling ("Explainable prediction of medical codes from clinical text"), large‑scale comparative effectiveness and safety studies of commonly used antihypertensives and other therapies ("Comprehensive comparative effectiveness and safety of first‑line antihypertensive drug classes", "Comparative first‑line effectiveness and safety of ACE inhibitors and angiotensin receptor blockers"), and a suite of practical tools and visualizations for improving data quality, pharmacovigilance, and clinician decision support (data quality tooling, DDI alert design studies, and ICU dashboard visualization). Across these areas he emphasizes reproducibility, transparency, and the translation of algorithmic advances into tools that stakeholders (researchers, clinicians, public health agencies) can adopt.

---

## Notable Works
- "Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (OHDSI): opportunities for observational researchers" (2015) — a foundational synthesis that articulates the vision and infrastructure for federated, reproducible observational research at population scale and helped catalyze broad community adoption of the OHDSI ecosystem.
- "Generating multi-label discrete patient records using generative adversarial networks" (2017) — introduced GAN‑based approaches to synthesize discrete, multi‑label EHR records, advancing methods for sharing realistic patient data while reducing privacy risk and enabling wider methodological development.
- "Explainable prediction of medical codes from clinical text" (2018) — developed explainable NLP models to predict diagnostic and procedure codes from clinical notes, addressing both performance and interpretability critical for clinical adoption and annotation workflows.

---

## Academic Background
Jon Duke is affiliated with **Georgia Tech**, where his scholarship bridges computer science, biomedical informatics, and clinical collaborations. Over the course of his career he has published extensively on topics that include common data models (OMOP), data quality tooling, synthetic data generation, clinical NLP, comparative effectiveness research, and clinical decision support. His work is highly cited (over 6,400 citations), reflecting broad influence across academic, clinical, and open‑source communities — notably the OHDSI network and multiple multicenter observational studies. Duke’s portfolio demonstrates sustained collaboration with academic medical centers, public health organizations, and tool builders (including contributions to open systems for data quality and pharmacovigilance), and his research has informed best practices for reproducible observational research, data interoperability, and trustworthy clinical analytics.

6wCEmNYAAAAJ.keywords: Algorithms, Theoretical Computer Science, Markov chains, Mixing times, Markov chain Monte Carlo, Statistical physics, Sampling algorithms, Glauber dynamics, Self-organizing particle systems, Lattice models
6wCEmNYAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Dana Randall is a **Professor** in the School of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she leads a research program in *algorithms and theoretical computer science*. Her lab brings together techniques from probability, combinatorics, and statistical physics to study fundamental algorithmic questions. Randall's work centers on the analysis and design of random processes—especially **Markov chain** methods—for sampling, counting, and optimization, and on applying those methods to problems ranging from lattice models in statistical mechanics to distributed *programmable matter* and *colloidal robotics*. Her research emphasizes rigorous understanding of <u>mixing behavior</u>, <u>convergence rates</u>, and <u>algorithmic sampling</u> for complex discrete systems.

---

## Research Areas
Randall's research spans several tightly connected areas in theoretical computer science and applied probability. A major thread is the rigorous analysis of Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithms: she has developed decomposition and comparison techniques to bound spectral gaps and mixing times (see "Markov chain decomposition for convergence rate analysis" and "Analyzing Glauber dynamics by comparison of Markov chains"), and has applied these tools to concrete combinatorial and physical models such as domino tilings, Ising and Potts systems, and hard-core lattice gases ("Markov chain algorithms for planar lattice structures", "Sampling spin configurations of an Ising system", "Phase Coexistence and Slow Mixing for the Hard-Core Model on ℤ2"). Complementing these theoretical contributions, Randall has worked on algorithmic and complexity aspects of sampling and counting (e.g., monomer-dimer coverings, triangulations) and on online algorithm problems ("Dynamic TCP acknowledgement and other stories about e/(e-1)"). More recently she has extended her probabilistic and MCMC expertise to distributed and engineered systems: designing and analyzing stochastic algorithms for *self-organizing particle systems* and *programmable matter* ("A Markov chain algorithm for compression in self-organizing particle systems", "A local stochastic algorithm for separation in heterogeneous self-organizing particle systems"), and exploring *colloidal robotics* and thermodynamic approaches to computation ("Programming active cohesive granular matter", "Thermodynamic computing", "Colloidal robotics").

---

## Notable Works
- Developed optimal randomized online strategies that generalize the classical ski-rental paradigm and resolve key online acknowledgment problems ("Dynamic TCP acknowledgement and other stories about e/(e-1)").
- Established powerful decomposition and comparison frameworks for analyzing the convergence rates of reversible Markov chains, enabling rigorous mixing time bounds for many natural chains ("Markov chain decomposition for convergence rate analysis"; "Analyzing Glauber dynamics by comparison of Markov chains").
- Applied MCMC methods to concrete lattice and sampling problems—proving rapid mixing results for planar tilings and establishing both positive and negative mixing-time phenomena for statistical-physics models (e.g., domino tilings, Ising, Potts, and hard-core models) ("Markov chain algorithms for planar lattice structures"; "Sampling spin configurations of an Ising system"; "Phase Coexistence and Slow Mixing for the Hard-Core Model on ℤ2").

---

## Academic Background
Dana Randall has a long-standing and highly cited career in theoretical computer science and algorithms, with publication activity extending from the late 1980s through the 2020s and a total citation record reflecting broad impact (total citations: 2658). Based at the Georgia Institute of Technology, she has built an interdisciplinary research agenda bridging theoretical foundations (algorithms, combinatorics, probabilistic analysis) and applied domains (statistical physics, programmable matter, robotics). Over decades of work she has collaborated widely across computer science and physics, produced foundational contributions to MCMC theory and practice, and translated those ideas into algorithmic frameworks for emerging engineered systems. Her program has trained and influenced many students and researchers in algorithmic theory, stochastic processes, and their applications.

## Overview
Hyesoon Kim is a Professor at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where she leads a research group focused on the intersection of **computer architecture** and **compiler/runtime systems**. Her work emphasizes *hardware–software co-design* for high-throughput and energy-efficient systems, with deep expertise in <u>GPU/GPGPU architectures</u>, <u>heterogeneous CPU–GPU systems</u>, and <u>memory-system optimizations</u>. Her research combines analytical modeling, architecture-aware compiler techniques, and system-level evaluation to expose and mitigate performance and power bottlenecks in modern parallel and data-intensive workloads.

---

## Research Areas
Hyesoon Kim’s research spans several tightly coupled areas in computer architecture and compilers. She is well known for analytical and simulation-based **GPU performance and power modeling** (e.g., analytical GPU models and McPAT-based GPU power models), which guide both architectural design and application tuning. Her work on **heterogeneous mapping and scheduling** (for example, the Qilin project) advances automatic distribution of computation across CPUs and GPUs. Memory-system research is a persistent theme: she has contributed models and policies for prefetching, TLP-aware cache management, die-stacked/3D memories, and unified memory management for GPUs (including investigations into page-fault overheads and DRAM-cache designs). More recent directions include **processing-in-memory (PIM)** and instruction-level offloading for graph workloads, practical studies of DNN deployment on edge devices, and security analyses of trusted execution (notably branch-shadowing attacks on SGX enclaves). Methodologically, her group produces both principled analytical frameworks and practical tools (e.g., the MacSim heterogeneous simulator and performance analysis frameworks) to connect microarchitectural mechanisms to real application behavior.

---

## Notable Works
- "An analytical model for a GPU architecture with memory-level and thread-level parallelism awareness" (2009) — A foundational analytical performance model that quantified how memory-level and thread-level parallelism interact on GPUs and became a reference point for later GPGPU performance studies.
- "Qilin: exploiting parallelism on heterogeneous multiprocessors with adaptive mapping" (2009) — Introduced a dynamic, adaptive mapping framework to automatically exploit CPU–GPU heterogeneity, improving performance and energy efficiency across diverse workloads.
- "An integrated GPU power and performance model" (2010) — Provided one of the early integrated approaches to jointly model GPU performance and power, enabling architecture and compiler designers to reason about trade-offs and optimize for energy-efficiency as well as throughput.

---

## Academic Background
Hyesoon Kim is a long-standing faculty member at the Georgia Institute of Technology with a prolific publication record and a total citation count exceeding 10k, reflecting sustained impact across architecture, compilers, and systems. Her scholarship has produced influential tools (e.g., MacSim) and high-impact papers across top architecture and systems venues, and her group collaborates broadly on problems ranging from security and edge inference to PIM and graph analytics. She serves the research community through program committees, invited talks, and mentoring graduate students and postdoctoral researchers; her body of work demonstrates consistent funding and industry engagement driving practical advances in heterogeneous and accelerator-rich computing.

## Overview
Brian Hrolenok is an **Assistant Professor (teaching)** in the Computer Science Department at **George Mason University** and leads an active research program bridging *machine learning*, *multiagent systems*, and *computational ethology*. His lab focuses on developing interpretable, executable models of collective behavior and on applying sensing and perception techniques from **computer vision** and **robotics** to problems in animal behavior and multiagent coordination. Central themes of his work include <u>constructing executable multiagent models from observation</u>, <u>tracking-driven inference of social structure</u>, and <u>sparse communication mechanisms for distributed coordination</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Hrolenok's research spans several overlapping areas: **multiagent systems** and agent-based simulation (both theoretical and applied), **machine learning** methods for modeling stochastic collective behavior, and the application of **computer vision** and sensing technologies for behavioral inference. From his early work on decentralized coordination — e.g., the use of movable beacons as a sparse analogue of pheromone-based communication — to later projects that convert dense tracking data into executable agent models, he emphasizes methods that translate observation into simulation-ready models. He routinely draws on probabilistic graphical models, sampling-based prediction techniques, and multi-target tracking approaches to infer social graphs and dominance relations in animal groups, and he investigates how learned models can be evaluated and validated against recorded behavior.

## Notable Works
- "Collaborative foraging using beacons" (2010, 38 citations) — Introduced a practical, sparse-beacon mechanism as a proxy for pheromone communication in multiagent foraging tasks, demonstrating how limited physical signaling can enable robust decentralized coordination.
- "Learning Executable Models of Multiagent Behavior from Live Animal Observation" (2013, 7 citations) — Presented methods to convert observational data into executable agent-based models suitable for simulation, enabling closer integration between empirical animal studies and computational simulation tools.
- "Sampling beats fixed estimate predictors for cloning stochastic behavior in multiagent systems" (2017, 7 citations) — Showed that sampling-based predictors more faithfully reproduce stochastic collective behaviors than fixed-estimate approaches, and framed fixed predictors within a probabilistic perspective to guide model selection.

## Academic Background
Based at George Mason University (personal homepage: https://cs.gmu.edu/~hrolenok/), Hrolenok has built a research profile at the intersection of computer science, robotics, and biology. His publication record (spanning 2010–2022) reflects a sustained engagement with the MASON multiagent simulation community and with interdisciplinary collaborations applying sensing and tracking (RFID, continuous 3D tracking, remote video) to infer social structures and dominance in animal groups. As an assistant professor on a teaching track, he combines course instruction with mentorship of student projects that deploy machine learning and simulation tools for real-world multiagent and ethological problems. His work has been cited 66 times, indicating ongoing impact in multiagent systems, learning-to-simulate approaches, and tracking-driven behavioral inference.
